questions.text: I believe that awareness of suffering creates a moral duty to act, not merely to acknowledge.
I find that I can discuss tragic events with analytical clarity without feeling personally implicated.
When I learn about someone's hardship, it shifts my priorities in a way I cannot easily override.
I believe that the most effective responses to crisis come from those who can suppress their emotional involvement.
I feel that indifference to others' suffering is a form of moral failure, regardless of the circumstances.
I am comfortable treating a person's emotional situation as a problem that requires strategy, not sympathy.
When I witness someone being dismissed or ignored in their pain, I feel compelled to intervene on their behalf.
I think that allowing suffering to influence your decision-making introduces bias that undermines good judgment.
I hold myself accountable when I fail to respond to suffering that I was aware of.
I prefer to base my responses to difficult situations on evidence and logic rather than on how the situation makes me feel.
I believe that the moral significance of another person's pain should override considerations of personal cost.
I have found that stepping back from the emotional content of a situation allows me to identify solutions others miss.
I feel a sustained motivation to help that persists long after the initial encounter with someone's suffering.
I think that effective leadership requires the ability to make decisions about people's lives without being swayed by their individual stories.
When I become aware that someone is struggling, I feel a moral responsibility to offer them support.
I approach others' emotional crises as situations that call for problem-solving rather than emotional participation.
I believe that a society's moral health can be measured by how seriously it takes the suffering of its most vulnerable members.
I am able to assess the severity of someone's situation without my assessment being colored by emotional involvement.
